one_line_takeaway: DP2-VL shows that pushing an image's post-projector VLM feature toward an antithetical (feature-negated) target via PGD-optimized imperceptible perturbations can drive identity/affiliation leakage from LoRA-fine-tuned VLMs down from ~80-96% ASR to as low as ~1-7% ASR at full protection ratio, but the defense degrades under low protection ratios and is substantially weakened by diffusion-based (DiffPure) purification.
key_finding: Without protection, LoRA fine-tuning on private photo datasets causes substantial identity/affiliation leakage: at protection ratio 0.0 (fully unprotected), ASRid/ASRaff values across scenarios range roughly from 80-96% under Brief prompts and are comparable or higher (up to 100% ASRaff for Couple) under Complex prompts. DP2-VL protection is strongly effective at high protection ratios: at ratio 1.0, ASRid drops to as low as 1.05-7.19% across scenarios under both prompt types (e.g., Identity ASRid falls from 94.00% at ratio 0.0 to 2.14% at ratio 1.0 under Brief prompts, and from 95.21% to 7.19% under Complex prompts). Protection strength scales roughly monotonically with protection ratio (0.0 to 1.0), remaining 'meaningful even at moderate protection ratios.' Robustness: the method tolerates Gaussian noise, resizing, and occlusion well (ASRid often stays under ~20%), but Gaussian blur is the most damaging post-processing operation -- 5x5 blur drives Identity ASRid up to 32.40% (Brief) / 38.60% (Complex), and the paper notes that under heavy blur the fine-tuned model tends to output memorized fine-tuning content regardless of the actual test image. Against purification defenses, JPEG compression only partially degrades protection (Identity ASRid 16.50% at Q=75, 24.40% at Q=50, Brief), while DiffPure is markedly more effective at removing protection (Identity ASRid 28.15% at S=100 rising further, 22.30% at S=250, Brief), i.e., diffusion purification is the strongest adaptive counter-measure tested. Cross-model generalization: protection generated per-target-model remains effective on Qwen-VL (Identity ASRid 3.12% Brief / 8.29% Complex) and MiniGPT-v2 (5.24% Brief / 11.15% Complex), both far below the unprotected LLaVA baselines, though MiniGPT-v2 consistently shows slightly higher residual leakage than Qwen-VL.
core_contribution: The paper makes two distinct contributions. (1) It defines and benchmarks a new privacy threat called 'identity-affiliation learning': an attacker LoRA-fine-tunes a vision-language model (VLM) on a small set of a target individual's private photos plus identity-revealing captions (including synthetically augmented images of the same identity), which embeds a covert 'privacy backdoor' so that later queries on other photos of the same person cause the model to disclose the person's name, property ownership, or social relationships. The authors build the first identity-affiliation benchmark dataset, covering 7 scenarios (Identity, Ownership, Classmate, Colleague, Couple, Family, Friend; 10 identities per scenario, ~25 images per identity, 230-300 samples per scenario) and show mainstream VLMs (LLaVA-v1.5-7B, Qwen-VL, MiniGPT-v2) can be fine-tuned to leak this information. (2) To defend against this, the paper proposes DP2-VL, described as 'the first Dataset Protection framework for private photos that leverages Data Poisoning.' DP2-VL adds an imperceptible per-image perturbation (bounded by an l-infinity constraint, epsilon = 8/255) that pushes each image's projected visual feature (post multimodal-projector embedding z = phi(E_v(x))) away from its own clean embedding and toward an 'antithetical' target obtained by L2-normalizing the negated clean embedding. Applied across a released dataset, this produces what the authors call a 'Global Feature Distribution Shift' (GFDS), intended to make the protected images resist being learned coherently during an attacker's subsequent LoRA fine-tuning.
threat_model: Three parties: (1) Photo Owner -- shares personal photos (with facial identity and often incidental captions) on open social platforms. (2) Malicious User/attacker -- collects the owner's public photos, optionally generates additional synthetic images of the same identity, auto-captions them with a VQA tool, augments captions with the owner's PII (name, property ownership, relationships), and jointly LoRA-fine-tunes an open VLM on image+identity-revealing-text pairs, then deploys the fine-tuned model via a public API. The attacker is explicitly stated to have white-box-style control over fine-tuning data and can also inject discriminatory lexical prefixes to later deflect blame onto the VLM's own 'jailbreak' behavior. (3) VLM User -- an ordinary API user, unaware of the embedded backdoor, who unknowingly triggers it by supplying other photos of the same target individual with identity-seeking prompts (e.g., 'Who is this person?'), causing the model to output the leaked identity/affiliation information. The defense (DP2-VL) assumes the photo owner (defender) can perturb their own photos with a bounded l-infinity perturbation before public release, with access to a vision encoder + multimodal projector to compute gradients (white-box access to at least a surrogate VLM feature extractor), and evaluates transfer to different target VLM architectures.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Section 7): (1) protection effectiveness depends heavily on protection ratio -- when a large fraction of clean (unprotected) samples remain in the released dataset, attackers can still partially recover identity-affiliation semantics, i.e., the defense is weak under low protection ratios / partial dataset coverage. (2) Purification-based preprocessing, especially diffusion-based purification (DiffPure), substantially reduces the induced feature shift and partially restores attack success, meaning an adaptive attacker aware of the defense can meaningfully weaken it. Additional points evident from the text but not framed as 'limitations' by the authors: the defense requires white-box (or surrogate) access to a vision encoder and multimodal projector to compute the perturbation, all evaluation is confined to LoRA fine-tuning as the attack vector (no full fine-tuning or other adaptation methods are tested), the benchmark dataset scale is modest (7 scenarios x 10 identities x ~25 images), and the threat-model framing conflates a plausible privacy-leakage attack with a specific narrative device (blame-shifting via injected lexical prefixes) that is asserted but not experimentally evaluated in the paper's results.

datasets_used: Identity-Affiliation Dataset (authors' own new benchmark): 7 scenarios (Identity, Ownership, Classmate, Colleague, Couple, Family, Friend), 10 identities per scenario, ~25 images per identity, mixing real and synthetically generated images; per-scenario sample counts: Identity 250, Ownership 280, Classmate 300, Colleague 240, Couple 230, Family 240, Friend 250
quantitative_results: Unprotected (ratio 0.0) Couple ASRaff: 96.16% (Brief), 100.00% (Complex) -- highest leakage observed
Fully protected (ratio 1.0) Friend ASRaff: 1.01% (Brief), 7.14% (Complex) -- among lowest residual leakage

Post-processing robustness (Identity ASRid, Brief prompts): Blur 3x3 = 15.20%, Blur 5x5 = 32.40% (most damaging), Noise sigma=5 = 20.09%, Noise sigma=10 = 15.00%, Occlusion = 6.67%, Resizing = 3.33%, Cropping = 18.89%
Purification robustness (Identity ASRid, Brief prompts): JPEG Q=50 = 24.40%, JPEG Q=75 = 16.50%, DiffPure S=100 = 28.15%, DiffPure S=250 = 22.30%
Cross-model generalizability (Identity ASRid, Brief prompts): Qwen-VL = 3.12%, MiniGPT-v2 = 5.24%; (Complex prompts): Qwen-VL = 8.29%, MiniGPT-v2 = 11.15%
PGD hyperparameters: epsilon = 8/255, alpha = 10, beta = 1.0, 1000 iterations; LoRA rank r = 128 on projection and attention layers; base model LLaVA-v1.5-7B
baselines_compared: No explicit prior-method baseline is compared numerically; the paper's comparison structure is internal, across protection ratios (0.0 = unprotected baseline through 1.0 = fully protected), across post-processing/purification conditions (unprocessed vs. blur/noise/resize/crop/occlusion vs. JPEG vs. DiffPure), and across target VLM architectures (LLaVA-v1.5-7B, Qwen-VL, MiniGPT-v2). Related prior data-poisoning-against-VLM works (e.g., Shadowcast, BadCLIP, TrojVLM, CleanCLIP) are discussed only in Related Work as motivating context, not re-implemented or benchmarked against DP2-VL in the experiments.
